# Introduction

We compare gaming behaviour between young people who are NEET (not in education, employment, or training; age ≤ 25) and a 1:1 propensity-score-matched sample of non-NEET players matched on age, gender, ethnicity, and country, using platform telemetry (Steam, Xbox, Nintendo) restricted to the three months before and after each participant's intake survey.

## Top games

The table below ranks the ten most-played games (by total playtime) separately for matched NEET and non-NEET players, alongside the number and percentage of each group who played the game, minutes per player, and genre metadata from IGDB.

::: {.cell-output-display}

## Time of day

The figure below shows each group's share of total playtime across hours of the day, indicating when NEET and non-NEET players tend to game.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Distribution of play across hours of the day for matched NEET and non-NEET players. Lines show each group's share of total playtime per hourly bin (timestamps in local time).](index_files/figure-docx/fig-time-of-day-1.png){#fig-time-of-day fig-align='center' width=80%}
:::
:::


## Distributions

The raincloud plots below compare the matched groups on six variables: objective playtime from telemetry, self-reported play, gaming disorder symptoms (GDT, wave 1), life satisfaction, and the perceived value of gaming for work and daily routines.


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![Raincloud plots comparing matched NEET and non-NEET players on objective playtime (telemetry), self-reported play, gaming disorder symptoms (GDT, wave 1), and life satisfaction (mean across waves). Dots show individual participants; boxes show IQR and median; densities show the distribution shape.](index_files/figure-docx/fig-distributions-1.png){#fig-distributions fig-align='center' width=80%}
:::
:::
